questions.question: Are disease models validated and predictive of human pathophysiology?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric supports evaluation of how well preclinical disease models represent human biology. It ensures models have mechanistic and phenotypic relevance to the human condition, which is critical for translatability, dose prediction, and confidence in preclinical efficacy signals.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and scientific best practices expect that sponsors demonstrate translational concordance between preclinical endpoints and human markers to support bridging preclinical findings to clinical outcomes. Failure to show concordance is a common contributor to clinical trial failure and regulatory skepticism of mechanistic claims. Sponsors must document comparative biomarker mapping, predictive value assessments, and cross-species validation in alignment with modern translational frameworks and inspection expectations.It ensures models have mechanistic and phenotypic relevance to the human condition, which is critical for translatability, dose prediction, and confidence in preclinical efficacy signals.

questions.question: Is there evidence of translational concordance between preclinical endpoints and human disease markers?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric guides assessment of whether nonclinical biomarkers or endpoints (e.g., gene expression, imaging, cytokines) translate to measurable and meaningful human clinical endpoints, supporting mechanistic bridging and regulatory confidence.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and scientific best practices expect that sponsors demonstrate translational concordance between preclinical endpoints and human markers to support bridging preclinical findings to clinical outcomes. Failure to show concordance is a common contributor to clinical trial failure and regulatory skepticism of mechanistic claims. Sponsors must document comparative biomarker mapping, predictive value assessments, and cross-species validation in alignment with modern translational frameworks and inspection expectations.

questions.question: Have humanized or orthotopic models been used for target engagement or efficacy studies?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric evaluates whether advanced models (e.g., humanized mice, orthotopic xenografts) are used to replicate human tissue microenvironments. These models can improve predictive validity for efficacy, immune response, and tumor biology, thus informing FIH planning and risk.
questions.rationale: Global regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) expect that when traditional models lack sufficient human relevance, sponsors select and validate advanced models (e.g., humanized, orthotopic) to improve the translatability of efficacy and target engagement data. Poorly chosen or poorly validated models can lead to false positives or negatives, causing clinical trial failure. Sponsors must demonstrate that model selection is risk-based, scientifically justified, and aligned with product MoA and disease biology — fully documented for inspection-readiness and scientific credibility in the domain of human relevance of preclinical models.

questions.question: Is there alignment between preclinical and clinical PK/PD?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures PK/PD relationships in animals (e.g., dose, Cmax, AUC vs. PD response) predict human exposure-response. It supports model-informed drug development (MIDD), first-in-human dose selection, and biomarker interpretation. Review of Translational Medicine Plan, PK/PD Strategy and Modeling Plan, Preclinical PK/PD Study Reports, Human PK/PD Data from early clinical studies (FIH, SAD/MAD), Allometric scaling analyses, Physiologically-Based PK (PBPK) models, PK/PD bridging analyses, Cross-species PK/PD concordance assessments, Target exposure vs. response analyses, Biomarker PK/PD linkage, Reverse translation of clinical data into model refinement, Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert reviews, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that preclinical PK/PD data and clinical PK/PD data have been systematically compared, that bridging analyses are documented, and that there is documented alignment or justified differences, supporting sound dose selection, exposure-response understanding, and regulatory expectations for human relevance of preclinical models
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) expect clear evidence that preclinical PK/PD data inform clinical dose selection and that there is systematic evaluation of alignment or gaps between preclinical and clinical PK/PD. Failure to demonstrate PK/PD bridging is a common cause of dosing errors, trial failure, or regulatory delays. Sponsors must document PK/PD bridging strategies, analyses, and governance oversight, maintaining transparent auditability in the human relevance of preclinical models domain.

questions.question: Is the MOA well understood and demonstrated in both preclinical models and human tissues?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric helps evaluate the biological plausibility and translational fidelity of the mechanism of action, ensuring the drug's effect can be traced from model systems to human tissues.  Review of Translational Medicine Plan, Mechanism of Action (MOA) Dossier or White Paper, Preclinical MOA Study Reports, Human tissue or cell-based assay data, Ex vivo/in vitro validation studies, Target expression profiling (preclinical vs. human tissues), Functional validation of target engagement in human-relevant models, Biomarker linkage to MOA, Reverse translation from clinical data, Scientific publications and literature review, Regulatory feedback on MOA evidence, External scientific expert/board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that the Mechanism of Action (MOA) is scientifically well-characterized, that its biological relevance and target engagement are demonstrated in both preclinical models and human tissues, and that the evidence is robust enough to support regulatory and scientific acceptance.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and modern translational frameworks expect sponsors to provide a scientifically sound, well-documented MOA that is validated in biologically relevant human tissues or cell models — not just in animals — to bridge target engagement, biomarker strategy, and clinical efficacy expectations. Poor MOA characterization is a frequent reason for regulatory pushback, trial failure, or inability to differentiate a product. Sponsors must show that preclinical models and human data provide concordant evidence of MOA.

questions.question: Are downstream pharmacologic effects measurable in both animals and humans?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric supports confidence in pharmacodynamic continuity, ensuring drug activity leads to measurable downstream responses across species. Review of Translational Medicine Plan, MOA Dossier, PK/PD Reports, Pharmacodynamic (PD) biomarker strategy documents, Preclinical PD Study Reports, Clinical PD Data and Biomarker Analysis, Cross-species biomarker mapping, Functional validation of PD endpoints, Exposure-response analyses (preclinical and clinical), Reverse translation from clinical data, Target pathway modulation studies, Regulatory feedback on PD strategy, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that downstream pharmacologic effects of the drug (aligned with MOA and disease biology) are quantitatively and qualitatively measurable in both preclinical models and human subjects, and that these effects are linked to dose/exposure and therapeutic intent, supporting translational confidence and regulatory expectations.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) expect that sponsors can demonstrate PD effects that bridge preclinical to clinical stages — beyond target engagement, showing functional modulation of downstream pathways or biomarkers relevant to MOA and disease outcomes. Lack of translatable PD markers contributes to uncertainty in dose selection, interpretation of clinical outcomes, and regulatory questions. Sponsors must document cross-species PD concordance, PD exposure-response relationships, and PD biomarker validation within the MOA translation domain.

questions.question: Has the target been confirmed in relevant human disease populations (e.g., biopsy, omics)?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric supports validation of the target's disease relevance by verifying its presence and modulation in human samples, increasing the credibility of the therapeutic hypothesis. Review of Translational Medicine Plan, MOA Dossier, Target Validation Reports, Human tissue expression studies (IHC, FISH, ISH, proteomics), Omics data analyses (genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics), Disease population stratification studies, Biopsy and biofluid biomarker studies, Public and proprietary human data sets (GTEx, TCGA, UK Biobank, etc.), Functional target modulation studies in disease-relevant human samples, Reverse translation from clinical data, Regulatory feedback on target validation, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that target expression, function, and modulation have been demonstrated in the intended human disease population(s) using biopsy data, omics data, or other disease-relevant human evidence, aligned with MOA and clinical development strategy.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and translational best practices expect sponsors to confirm target relevance in intended patient populations — particularly where patient selection, stratification, or biomarker-driven development is planned. Lack of human disease population validation is a frequent cause of regulatory skepticism, failed trials, and poor precision medicine positioning. Sponsors must document that target validation is grounded in human data, with scientific rigor and inspection-ready traceability in the MOA translation domain.

questions.question: Are translatable pharmacodynamic markers linked to MOA?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures there are measurable, translatable biomarkers that respond to the drug in a manner consistent with its proposed mechanism, bridging animal and human data. Review of Translational Medicine Plan, MOA Dossier, PD Biomarker Strategy Documents, Preclinical PD Study Reports, Clinical PD Data, Biomarker Mapping Reports (preclinical → clinical), Mechanistic linkage of PD markers to MOA, Functional validation studies, Exposure-response analyses, Reverse translation from clinical data to biomarker refinement, Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that pharmacodynamic (PD) markers with translatable relevance (preclinical → clinical) are clearly linked mechanistically to the MOA, are quantitatively validated, and are being used to support dose selection, efficacy interpretation, and benefit-risk assessment aligned with regulatory expectations.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) expect sponsors to demonstrate that PD markers are mechanistically linked to MOA, validated across species where used, and serve as a bridge between preclinical and clinical findings. Lack of validated, translatable PD markers contributes to uncertain dose selection, poor trial interpretability, and regulatory risk. Sponsors must document a PD biomarker strategy, with scientific justification, mechanistic alignment, and inspection-ready traceability in the MOA translation domain.

questions.question: Is there a written, cross-functional biomarker strategy covering discovery to validation?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric evaluates whether the biomarker plan is comprehensive, forward-looking, and aligned with clinical development and regulatory expectations. Review of Biomarker Strategy Document or Translational Medicine Plan, Cross-functional biomarker governance structure, Discovery to validation workflow documentation, Integration of biomarker strategy with clinical development plan, Preclinical biomarker data, Clinical biomarker plans, Biomarker Qualification Plans (internal or formal for regulatory submission), Target linkage documentation, Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) sections covering biomarkers, QA-reviewed version-controlled documents, Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that a written, cross-functional biomarker strategy exists, spanning discovery through preclinical and clinical validation, is aligned with MOA and clinical development, is governed cross-functionally, and meets scientific, operational, and regulatory expectations.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and translational best practices expect sponsors to develop a robust, documented biomarker strategy that aligns with product biology, MOA, and intended clinical use (PD, predictive, prognostic, enrichment, companion diagnostic). Ad hoc biomarker work or lack of governance is a common source of regulatory findings, uninterpretable trial results, and failed translational strategies. A written, cross-functional biomarker strategy is an essential translational governance artifact that must be version-controlled, inspected, and aligned with the overall product strategy.

questions.question: Are biomarkers aligned with TPP and clinical development goals (diagnostic, predictive, PD, safety)?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures biomarker activities are integrated into program strategy and address the right questions for patient selection, safety monitoring, and regulatory success. Review of Biomarker Strategy Document, Translational Medicine Plan, Target Product Profile (TPP), Clinical Development Plan (CDP), MOA Dossier, Biomarker classification and intended use documentation (diagnostic, predictive, PD, safety), Clinical protocol and SAP alignment, Regulatory feedback and Scientific Advice, External expert input, Biomarker qualification pathway (if applicable), Reverse translation from clinical data, Governance Committee/Board minutes, QA-reviewed version-controlled documents, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that biomarkers selected and developed are explicitly aligned to the Target Product Profile (TPP) and to clinical development goals, with clear classification (diagnostic, predictive, PD, safety), operational integration in trials, and regulatory readiness, fully traceable and inspectable within the Biomarker Strategy domain
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and translational best practices expect that biomarker strategy is integrated with clinical development goals and TPP — not an isolated activity — and that intended use is clearly defined (diagnostic, predictive, PD, safety). Lack of this alignment results in regulatory queries, uncertain trial outcomes, and failed biomarker-driven strategies. Sponsors must document biomarker alignment to product value proposition, clinical trial objectives, and regulatory strategy.

questions.question: Are biomarkers included in clinical protocols and sample collection plans?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric confirms whether biomarker execution is embedded in clinical operations, ensuring sample availability for future analysis and interpretation.  Review of Clinical Protocols, SAPs, Sample Collection Plans / Laboratory Manuals, Translational Medicine Plan, Biomarker Strategy Document, Biomarker CRFs and eCRF fields, Sample accountability tracking and chain of custody documentation, Informed Consent Forms (ICFs) — biomarker language and patient information, Pre-analytical handling procedures, Assay readiness documentation, Vendor qualification and oversight records, Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that biomarkers are explicitly included in clinical protocols and sample collection plans, with operational readiness, governance oversight, and regulatory compliance, enabling high-quality, analyzable data aligned with the Biomarker Strategy and clinical development objectives.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and translational best practices expect that clinical biomarker activities are explicitly and traceably embedded in clinical protocols and sample management plans, with clear patient consent, operational rigor, and analytical readiness. Failure to operationalize biomarker strategy leads to non-evaluable samples, loss of data integrity, regulatory inspection findings, and trial failure risk. Sponsors must document that biomarkers move from strategy → operational plans → clinical execution → analysis → regulatory submission in an inspectable manner.

questions.question: Are preanalytical variables and sample logistics addressed (collection, handling, storage)?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric safeguards data integrity by ensuring that the quality of biospecimens is maintained through standardized preanalytical processes.Review of Sample Collection Plans, Laboratory Manuals, Preanalytical SOPs, Sample handling and processing workflows, Preanalytical variable control matrices, Chain of custody records, Sample tracking system outputs, Vendor qualification for sample logistics, Pre-study vendor feasibility and process validation reports, Stability data for biomarkers (short- and long-term), Cold chain and storage condition validation, Sample reconciliation reports, Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that preanalytical variables (collection timepoints, tube type, processing time, temperature, freeze-thaw cycles, storage conditions) and sample logistics (collection, handling, shipping, storage) are explicitly defined, validated, and operationally controlled, supporting high-quality biomarker data generation and regulatory acceptance.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA), GCP (ICH E6), and translational best practices stress that preanalytical variability is a major source of biomarker data noise and that preanalytical controls and logistics validation are required to produce regulatory-grade data. Lack of control over these variables is a common reason for inconsistent biomarker results, failed analyses, inspection findings, and trial risk. Sponsors must document a fully controlled and auditable preanalytical and logistics strategy.

questions.question: Are PD biomarkers available to confirm target engagement in vivo?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures pharmacodynamic biomarkers provide evidence of target engagement in humans, supporting dose and exposure justification. Review of Translational Medicine Plan, MOA Dossier, PD Biomarker Strategy Document, Preclinical PD biomarker validation reports, Clinical PD biomarker data (FIH, SAD/MAD, expansion cohorts), Biomarker qualification documentation, MOA and target engagement linkage reports, Exposure-response analyses, Cross-species biomarker concordance reports, Reverse translation analyses, Clinical Protocols and SAPs, Informed Consent documents (ICF), Regulatory feedback and Scientific Advice, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that PD biomarkers are available, validated, and operationalized to confirm target engagement in vivo, that they are mechanistically linked to MOA, quantitatively analyzable, and integrated into clinical development, enabling dose selection, benefit-risk assessment, and regulatory alignment.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and translational best practices expect that sponsors demonstrate target engagement in vivo using validated PD biomarkers, providing mechanistic evidence to support dose/exposure-response relationships and clinical relevance of pharmacologic activity. Absence of validated PD biomarkers weakens dose selection rationale, impairs regulatory submissions, and contributes to trial failure. Sponsors must document a rigorous, auditable PD biomarker framework aligned with MOA and clinical development goals.

questions.question: Are dynamic changes in PD biomarkers characterized across doses and time?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric supports the assessment of biomarker responsiveness and kinetics, enabling modeling and real-time pharmacologic monitoring.  Review of Translational Medicine Plan, PD Biomarker Strategy Document, Preclinical and Clinical PD Biomarker Reports, PK/PD modeling reports (including time-course and dose-response modeling), Exposure-response and time-response analyses, Longitudinal biomarker datasets, Statistical Analysis Plans (SAPs) with longitudinal analysis plans, Graphical outputs (e.g., time-course plots, waterfall plots, spaghetti plots), Variability analyses (intra-subject, inter-subject, inter-dose), Cross-species temporal concordance, Reverse translation analyses, Regulatory feedback and Scientific Advice, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that dynamic changes in PD biomarkers are characterized over time and across dose levels, that this is used to support exposure-response relationships, dose optimization, and clinical decision-making, and that it meets scientific rigor and regulatory expectations.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and translational best practices expect that PD biomarker data is analyzed not just as static snapshots, but as dynamic measures of drug pharmacology — both across doses and over time. Lack of time-course characterization weakens dose selection, benefit-risk assessments, and mechanistic claims. Sponsors must demonstrate that PD biomarkers show dose- and time-dependent changes, aligned with MOA and clinical pharmacology.

questions.question: Are PD markers measurable in accessible human matrices (blood, urine, CSF, etc.)?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric evaluates practicality and feasibility of sampling, critical for implementation in clinical protocols and reducing patient burden. Review of Translational Medicine Plan, PD Biomarker Strategy Document, Preclinical and Clinical PD Biomarker Reports, Analytical validation reports for each matrix (blood, plasma, serum, urine, CSF, saliva, tissue biopsies), Limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), matrix effect evaluations, Stability reports (short-term, freeze-thaw, long-term) for each matrix, Bioanalytical method validation reports (FDA, EMA-compliant), Clinical Protocols and SAPs specifying matrix/sample type, Sample collection SOPs, Sample handling and processing SOPs, Laboratory manuals, Vendor qualification and oversight documentation, Regulatory feedback and Scientific Advice, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that PD markers are measurable with sufficient analytical sensitivity, specificity, and robustness in accessible human matrices, and that they are operationalized for routine use in clinical trials, aligned with scientific, regulatory, and operational expectations.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA), ICH, and CIOMS XI expect that PD biomarkers be measurable in accessible human matrices (e.g. blood, urine, CSF), with fully validated methods, ensuring data integrity, reproducibility, and regulatory compliance. Failure to validate matrix-specific performance leads to non-acceptable biomarker data, regulatory delays, or unusable trial results. Sponsors must demonstrate matrix validation, assay readiness, and operational control for PD biomarker measurements.

questions.question: Are biomarker assays qualified or validated to support decision-making?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures PD biomarker data is reliable and actionable, meeting analytical standards for use in regulatory or clinical decision-making.  Review of Biomarker Strategy Document, Translational Medicine Plan, Assay qualification reports, Assay validation reports (analytical performance: accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, robustness, reproducibility), Bioanalytical method validation reports (FDA/EMA-compliant for clinical use), Fit-for-purpose validation documentation (based on intended use: exploratory, pharmacodynamic, diagnostic, predictive, companion diagnostic), Sample stability and matrix effect evaluations, Performance characterization across intended clinical use (dose selection, PK/PD modeling, efficacy interpretation, regulatory submission), Clinical Protocols and SAPs specifying decision contexts, Vendor qualification and oversight reports, Regulatory feedback and Scientific Advice, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that biomarker assays are qualified or validated appropriately to support intended clinical and regulatory decision-making, consistent with their intended use and regulatory expectations, and are fully operationalized and inspectable.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA), ICH E16, and CIOMS XI expect that biomarker assays used for decision-making (dose selection, stage gate progression, efficacy interpretation, benefit-risk assessments) be fit-for-purpose qualified or fully validated, depending on use. Inadequate assay qualification is a frequent cause of regulatory rejection, poor trial interpretability, and strategic risk. Sponsors must demonstrate that biomarker assays are rigorously qualified/validated, traceable, and aligned to intended decision points.

questions.question: Are biomarkers identified to stratify or enrich clinical populations?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric assesses whether biomarker use can improve trial efficiency, increase signal detection, and guide personalized medicine strategies.  Review of Biomarker Strategy Document, Translational Medicine Plan, MOA Dossier, Predictive Biomarker Discovery Reports, Biomarker Qualification Plans, Retrospective/prospective clinical data analyses (biomarker-treatment interaction), Preclinical and human disease tissue expression data, Omics analyses (genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics), Literature reviews and external benchmarking, Biomarker-driven patient selection rationale in Clinical Development Plan (CDP), Clinical Protocols and SAPs incorporating stratification/enrichment, Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, Companion Diagnostic (CDx) development plans (if applicable), External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that biomarkers are identified and supported by scientific, clinical, and regulatory evidence to be used for stratifying or enriching clinical trial populations, aligned with MOA, TPP, and clinical development goals.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) expect clear scientific and clinical rationale for biomarker-driven stratification or enrichment of clinical populations. Poorly substantiated biomarker selection results in regulatory rejection, trial failure, or uninterpretable efficacy/safety signals. Sponsors must rigorously document identification, validation, and clinical integration of stratification biomarkers — especially where biomarkers impact eligibility, randomization, or regulatory labeling.

questions.question: Are genetic or protein expression markers associated with response or resistance?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Biomarker Strategy Document, Translational Medicine Plan, MOA Dossier, Genomic and transcriptomic profiling reports (WGS, WES, RNA-seq, gene panels), Proteomic profiling reports (mass spec, Olink, SomaScan, RPPA), Target expression studies (IHC, immunofluorescence, flow cytometry), Retrospective responder vs non-responder analyses, Statistical modeling of biomarker-treatment interactions (interaction terms, hazard ratios, ORs), Multi-omic integrative analyses, Literature reviews and external benchmarking, Human disease tissue expression data, Clinical Protocols and SAPs defining biomarker hypotheses and exploratory/confirmatory endpoints, Companion Diagnostic (CDx) development plans (if applicable), Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that genetic or protein expression markers have been identified and supported by scientific and clinical data as being associated with response or resistance to the investigational agent, and that these markers are integrated into clinical development and regulatory strategy where appropriate.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA), CIOMS XI, and ICH E16 expect that sponsors rigorously validate genetic/protein biomarkers associated with response or resistance, and appropriately operationalize them in clinical development and regulatory filings. Failure to do so results in regulatory rejection of biomarker claims, uninterpretable trial results, and labeling limitations. Sponsors must demonstrate scientific validity, clinical utility, and regulatory readiness of such biomarkers — especially if used for stratification, enrichment, or label claims.

questions.question: Has biomarker prevalence in the target population been estimated or confirmed?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric supports feasibility assessments for recruitment and commercialization by ensuring biomarkers are sufficiently prevalent in the intended population. Review of Biomarker Strategy Document, Translational Medicine Plan, MOA Dossier, Biomarker prevalence studies (epidemiology reports, registry analyses, clinical trial screening logs, commercial testing data), Human tissue expression data (IHC, ISH, flow cytometry, RNA-seq), Genomic/proteomic database analyses (TCGA, GTEx, COSMIC, public/private datasets), Retrospective analyses from prior clinical trials, Literature review of prevalence estimates, Geographic/ethnic/age/sex subgroup analyses, Clinical Protocols and CDP accounting for prevalence in statistical assumptions (powering, screen failure rates, recruitment feasibility), Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that biomarker prevalence in the intended target population has been systematically estimated or empirically confirmed, and that this has been incorporated into clinical trial design, regulatory strategy, and operational planning.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA) and CIOMS XI expect sponsors to provide robust estimates of biomarker prevalence in the intended clinical trial and treatment populations. This informs trial feasibility, power calculations, risk of screen failure, and applicability of trial results to clinical practice. Lack of prevalence data results in trial feasibility risk, protocol amendments, and regulatory questioning of representativeness. Sponsors must show that prevalence has been rigorously estimated, with traceable evidence.

questions.question: Are patient selection criteria biomarker-driven in clinical trials?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric assesses whether biomarker-positive patients are prospectively selected for inclusion, improving study power and regulatory precision.  Review of Biomarker Strategy Document, Translational Medicine Plan, Clinical Development Plan (CDP), Clinical Protocols and SAPs, Inclusion/exclusion criteria linked to biomarkers, Randomization stratification plans, Biomarker eligibility testing algorithms (central/local labs), Companion Diagnostic (CDx) development and regulatory status (if applicable), Cutoff determination reports for biomarkers used in selection, Retrospective responder vs non-responder data supporting biomarker use, Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert/scientific board input, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that patient selection criteria in clinical trials are explicitly biomarker-driven where applicable, and that biomarker use is based on scientific rationale, clinical data, regulatory alignment, and operational feasibility.
questions.rationale: Regulators (EMA, FDA, PMDA), ICH E16, CIOMS XI, and FDA Enrichment Guidance expect that when biomarkers are used to drive patient selection, this is rigorously justified, operationalized, and documented in clinical trial design. Poorly justified or operationalized biomarker-driven selection leads to regulatory rejection, invalid trial results, and non-approvable label claims. Sponsors must demonstrate clear linkage between biomarker data and eligibility criteria and ensure full traceability.

questions.question: Are biomarkers in place to predict or monitor potential toxicities (e.g., cardiac, hepatic, renal)?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric evaluates whether safety risks are monitored proactively using translational biomarkers, improving patient protection and data quality.
questions.rationale: Regulators (FDA, EMA, PMDA), CIOMS XI, ICH E16 expect that sponsors identify mechanistically plausible and clinically actionable safety biomarkers where available — especially in high-risk organ systems (cardiac, hepatic, renal, neurologic). Lack of safety biomarkers is a frequent cause of regulatory requests and can lead to unanticipated toxicities and trial holds. Safety biomarker strategy must be traceable to MOA and toxicology signals.

questions.question: Are translational links established between nonclinical toxicity findings and human safety biomarkers?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures preclinical safety signals are bridged to human biology through shared biomarkers, reducing FIH risk.  Review of Translational Medicine Plan, Safety Biomarker Strategy Document, Nonclinical GLP and non-GLP toxicity reports, Mechanistic toxicology studies, Exploratory biomarker studies in animal models, PK/PD-Toxicity models, Exposure margin calculations (AUC, Cmax vs NOAEL), Histopathology and biomarker concordance analyses, Cross-species comparison of biomarker dynamics (animal to human), Biomarker validation reports (translation feasibility), Clinical Protocols and SAPs specifying biomarker use informed by nonclinical findings, Regulatory Scientific Advice/Feedback, External expert/scientific board input, Literature reviews on translational relevance, Governance Committee/Board minutes, TMF/ISF documentation, QA Audit Reports, audit trails — to confirm that translational links between nonclinical toxicity findings and human safety biomarkers are clearly established, documented, and operationalized in clinical trials, aligned with regulatory expectations and best practices.
questions.rationale: Regulators (FDA, EMA, PMDA), CIOMS XI, ICH E16, and the PSTC (Predictive Safety Testing Consortium) expect sponsors to demonstrate that preclinical toxicity signals are translated to clinically meaningful biomarkers for monitoring and mitigation in human trials. Lack of such translational links is a common reason for clinical hold requests, trial suspensions, and regulatory delay. It is critical that these links be prospectively planned and traceable.

questions.question: Are safety markers validated and acceptable to regulators or industry standards (e.g., FDA BEST)?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric assesses whether biomarkers used for safety meet regulatory acceptance criteria and can support decision-making or labeling. Review of TM Plan, Safety Biomarker Strategy, validation reports, regulatory feedback, PSTC/FDA BEST alignment, protocol/SAP alignment, external input, QA and TMF documentation.
questions.rationale: Regulators (FDA/EMA) expect that safety biomarkers used for clinical decisions are validated to regulatory or industry standards (e.g. FDA BEST, PSTC), with traceable evidence supporting reliability and interpretability.

questions.question: Are assays (e.g., ELISA, qPCR, flow cytometry, LC-MS, NGS) developed and technically validated?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric confirms whether biomarker assays meet analytical validation criteria necessary for reliable deployment in clinical studies. Review of TM Plan, Biomarker Assay Development Plan, assay validation reports (GxP or fit-for-purpose), assay SOPs, bioanalytical method validation reports (FDA/EMA), regulatory feedback, external expert input, QA reports, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulators (FDA, EMA, PMDA) and industry best practice require biomarker assays used in clinical trials to be technically validated (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, reproducibility), to ensure data reliability and regulatory acceptability.

questions.question: Are performance characteristics (sensitivity, specificity, LLOQ, ULOQ) documented?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures analytical parameters required by ICH and regulatory authorities are documented and meet fit-for-purpose criteria. Review of TM Plan, Biomarker Assay Development Plan, assay validation reports (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, LLOQ, ULOQ), method validation reports (FDA/EMA), SOPs, QA reports, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory standards (FDA/EMA/ICH) require that assay performance characteristics be fully documented to support biomarker data reliability for clinical and regulatory use.

questions.question: Is there a clear plan for bioanalytical method validation under GxP where required?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric addresses whether method validation follows GxP requirements for clinical trial use, supporting audit readiness. Review of TM Plan, Biomarker Assay Development Plan, Bioanalytical Method Validation Plan, QA-approved SOPs, validation protocols (GxP or fit-for-purpose), regulatory feedback, governance reviews, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: For regulatory acceptance, sponsors must document a clear, prospectively approved plan for GxP method validation where required (FDA, EMA, ICH), aligned to clinical use and regulatory expectations.

questions.question: Are biomarker assays transferable to central labs or CROs?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric supports scalability and implementation by assessing whether assays can be consistently reproduced outside of R&D settings. Review of TM Plan, Biomarker Assay Development Plan, assay transfer protocols, tech transfer reports, vendor qualification documents, bridging/transfer validation data, central lab/CRO contracts and SOPs, regulatory feedback, TMF/ISF completeness
questions.rationale: Regulatory standards and operational best practice require documented assay transfer processes to ensure reproducibility and integrity of biomarker data when assays are used across central labs or CROs in multicenter trials.

questions.question: Are biospecimen collection protocols in place with informed consent and ethics approvals?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures regulatory compliance and ethical execution of biospecimen collection, supporting downstream biomarker analysis.Review of TM Plan, Sample Management Plan, biospecimen collection protocols, informed consent templates, ethics approvals, biorepository SOPs, sample tracking systems (LIMS), chain-of-custody SOPs, integrity monitoring plans, vendor qualifications, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: For compliance with FDA/EMA/ICH/CIOMS, biospecimen collection, storage, tracking, and integrity must be controlled, traceable, and aligned with consent/ethics approvals to ensure data validity, regulatory compliance, and subject protection.

questions.question: Are biorepositories or sample tracking systems implemented?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric confirms sample chain-of-custody and traceability, critical for quality control and retrospective analyses. Review of TM Plan, Sample Management Plan, biospecimen collection protocols, informed consent templates, ethics approvals, biorepository SOPs, sample tracking systems (LIMS), chain-of-custody SOPs, integrity monitoring plans, vendor qualifications, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: For compliance with FDA/EMA/ICH/CIOMS, biospecimen collection, storage, tracking, and integrity must be controlled, traceable, and aligned with consent/ethics approvals to ensure data validity, regulatory compliance, and subject protection.

questions.question: Are sample integrity and chain-of-custody procedures established?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric assesses the reliability and traceability of samples across their lifecycle to ensure assay integrity. Review of TM Plan, Sample Management Plan, biospecimen collection protocols, informed consent templates, ethics approvals, biorepository SOPs, sample tracking systems (LIMS), chain-of-custody SOPs, integrity monitoring plans, vendor qualifications, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: For compliance with FDA/EMA/ICH/CIOMS, biospecimen collection, storage, tracking, and integrity must be controlled, traceable, and aligned with consent/ethics approvals to ensure data validity, regulatory compliance, and subject protection.

questions.question: Are nonclinical and translational data sufficient to justify FIH starting dose and escalation?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric evaluates whether MABEL, NOAEL, and other translational data support safe, rational first-in-human dosing. Review of TM Plan, FIH Dose Justification Package, MABEL/Human Equivalent Dose (HED) calculations, NOAEL-based starting dose, PK/PD modeling reports, safety margins, allometric scaling reports, translational PK/PD concordance, MOA-based risk assessment, regulatory feedback, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies (FDA/EMA/PMDA) require that FIH starting dose and escalation decisions be fully justified by nonclinical and translational data to ensure patient safety and scientific validity. Lack of robust justification is a major cause of IND/CTA clinical hold.

questions.question: Are PK/PD relationships modeled and simulated for dose selection?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures that model-informed drug development principles are applied to improve dose predictions. Review of TM Plan, PK/PD modeling reports, exposure-response simulations, translational PK/PD concordance, MABEL justification, dose-response modeling, interspecies scaling, safety margin modeling, regulatory feedback, governance review, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: FDA, EMA, and ICH expect sponsors to model PK/PD relationships to support rational FIH dose selection and escalation. Failure to model and simulate exposure-response relationships is a frequent cause of regulatory challenge or clinical hold.

questions.question: Is there confidence in pharmacologic activity at clinically achievable exposures?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric assesses if predicted human exposures are sufficient to achieve pharmacologic effect, de-risking early trials. Review of TM Plan, FIH Dose Justification Package, PK/PD modeling reports, translational PD marker data, MOA-based PD markers, exposure-response analyses, clinical exposure projections, target coverage modeling, regulatory feedback, governance review, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: FDA, EMA, and ICH expect demonstration that clinically achievable exposures will yield pharmacologically meaningful activity based on translational and PK/PD data. Failure to demonstrate this is a key reason for FIH delays, holds, or inability to progress beyond initial cohorts.

questions.question: Are FIH biomarkers integrated into early clinical endpoints?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric ensures biomarker endpoints are included in early clinical studies to evaluate pharmacology and guide escalation. Review of TM Plan, Biomarker Strategy, FIH Protocol and SAP, translational PD marker data, PK/PD modeling reports, clinical endpoint definitions, biomarker assay validation reports, sample collection plans, regulatory feedback, governance review, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies (FDA, EMA, ICH) expect that translational biomarkers that inform pharmacologic activity and early clinical response are prospectively integrated into FIH trials. This ensures meaningful interpretation of early data and supports informed dose escalation and program decisions. Failure to integrate biomarkers is a common reason for FIH trials lacking interpretable early signals.

questions.question: Is a PK/PD or QSP model in place to guide dose and regimen selection?
questions.rubric_tests: This rubric supports evaluation of how well preclinical disease models represent human biology. It ensures models have mechanistic and phenotypic relevance to the human condition, which is critical for translatability, dose prediction, and confidence in preclinical efficacy signals. Review of TM Plan, QSP/PK/PD modeling reports, model qualification and validation reports, exposure-response simulations, dose-exposure-response projections, scenario analyses, MOA-based systems models, time-course simulations, regulatory feedback, governance review, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: FDA, EMA, and ICH increasingly expect that dose and regimen selection is supported by robust PK/PD or Quantitative Systems Pharmacology (QSP) modeling. These models inform optimal dosing strategies, reduce patient risk, and support regulatory submissions. Lack of modeling can lead to suboptimal dosing decisions, regulatory challenge, or trial failure.

questions.question: Are physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models used to predict human exposure?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Modeling & Simulation Plan, PBPK model reports, model qualification and validation reports, sensitivity analyses, scenario testing, human exposure predictions, scaling from preclinical to human, formulation and food effect predictions, drug-drug interaction (DDI) simulations, population variability modeling, special population (renal/hepatic impairment, pediatrics) simulations, clinical protocol and SAP alignment, regulatory feedback, governance review, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies (FDA, EMA, PMDA) increasingly expect that PBPK modeling is used to bridge preclinical data to human exposure predictions, to inform FIH dosing, to assess food effects, DDIs, and to support special populations. Robust PBPK modeling reduces the risk of unexpected clinical exposures, optimizes trial design, and strengthens regulatory submissions. Poor or absent PBPK modeling is a frequent reason for FDA/EMA questions or post-IND/CTA deficiencies.

questions.question: Are modeling efforts cross-validated with preclinical and clinical data?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Modeling & Simulation Plan, PK/PD, PBPK, QSP model reports, model qualification and validation reports, cross-validation strategy and results, use of independent preclinical datasets, translational data alignment, use of clinical trial data for validation, predictive checks, sensitivity analyses, documentation of data sources, regulatory feedback, governance review, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Cross-validation with both preclinical and clinical data is critical to build confidence in the predictive performance of quantitative models. Regulatory agencies expect evidence that models are not simply trained on a single dataset but are externally qualified using independent data, both from preclinical studies (PK/PD, biomarker, exposure-response) and early clinical trials. Lack of cross-validation undermines model credibility and is a frequent cause of regulatory pushback on modeling-based dosing recommendations or label claims

questions.question: Are simulations used to inform adaptive designs or stopping criteria?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Modeling & Simulation Plan, simulation reports for adaptive design, clinical protocol and SAP alignment, governance review records, sensitivity and scenario analyses, simulations linked to stopping rules, dose-escalation rules, interim decision points, exposure-response predictions, Bayesian and frequentist adaptive design support, FDA/EMA briefing materials, regulatory feedback, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies encourage the use of simulations to inform adaptive clinical trial designs and stopping criteria. Modeling and simulation can optimize trial efficiency, enhance patient safety, and improve decision quality. Failure to use simulations to support adaptive decisions and stopping criteria increases the risk of inefficient designs, regulatory challenges, and poor benefit-risk management during trials.

questions.question: Are trials designed to incorporate or adapt to biomarker results (e.g., enrichment, basket, umbrella)?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Biomarker Strategy, Clinical Development Plan, Modeling & Simulation Plan, clinical protocol and SAP, enrichment and stratification plans, basket/umbrella trial designs, adaptive biomarker-driven designs, governance review records, simulation outputs linked to biomarker strategy, translational concordance assessments, biomarker assay validation reports, regulatory feedback, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies strongly encourage biomarker-driven clinical trial designs where supported by MOA and translational data. Biomarker-enriched, basket, and umbrella trials can improve efficacy signals, reduce trial size, and support precision medicine strategies. Failure to design trials that appropriately incorporate biomarker results when the biology supports it is a common cause of negative trials, regulatory questions, and missed opportunities for accelerated pathways.

questions.question: Are exploratory vs. hypothesis-driven biomarker endpoints clearly distinguished?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Biomarker Strategy, Clinical Development Plan, Clinical Protocol and SAP, endpoint hierarchy, biomarker endpoint definitions, statistical analysis plans for biomarker endpoints, modeling & simulation plans (where applicable), governance review records, translational concordance assessments, assay validation reports, regulatory feedback, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies expect that exploratory and hypothesis-driven biomarker endpoints are clearly distinguished and that statistical plans are appropriately aligned. Failure to do so leads to misinterpretation of trial results, potential regulatory rejection of biomarker claims, and inspection findings. Ambiguous endpoint definitions can also create legal/commercial risks when biomarkers are linked to drug claims or labeling.

questions.question: Are statistical plans in place for biomarker subgroup analyses?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Biomarker Strategy, Clinical Development Plan, Clinical Protocol and SAP, pre-specification of biomarker subgroup analyses, statistical methodology for subgroup analysis, multiplicity control plan, Modeling & Simulation Plan (where applicable), sensitivity analysis plan, regulatory feedback, governance review records, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies expect that if biomarker subgroup analyses are planned or anticipated, they are pre-specified in the SAP with appropriate statistical methodology and multiplicity control. Post-hoc or exploratory subgroup findings are subject to scrutiny if used for regulatory decisions or labeling. Lack of clear statistical planning for subgroup analyses is a frequent cause of FDA/EMA criticism and can invalidate key claims.

questions.question: Are companion diagnostics (CDx) being developed, if required?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Biomarker Strategy, CDx development plan, Clinical Development Plan, Clinical Protocol, SAP, Diagnostic Regulatory Plan, assay validation plan, analytical validation reports, clinical validation reports, design control and quality documentation, regulatory engagement records, IDE/PMA submissions (US), IVDR compliance (EU), governance review records, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness
questions.rationale: When companion diagnostics (CDx) are required to support drug approval and use, regulatory agencies expect an integrated CDx and drug development strategy. FDA (via PMA or de novo pathway), EMA, and other agencies require clinical validation of the CDx in the same patient population as the therapeutic. Failure to align CDx development with drug development leads to delays, regulatory rejection, and inability to commercialize precision medicine products

questions.question: Are biomarker strategies aligned with regulatory expectations (FDA, EMA, PMDA)?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Biomarker Strategy, Regulatory Strategy Plan, Clinical Development Plan, Clinical Protocol, SAP, pre-IND / pre-CTA briefing documents, agency meeting minutes, feedback responses, biomarker validation plan, regulatory precedent reviews, alignment with relevant FDA, EMA, PMDA guidance documents, labeling strategy, governance review records, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies expect biomarker strategies to be aligned with evolving expectations for validation, use in clinical trial designs, labeling, and approval pathways. Misalignment results in regulatory questions, delay of trials or submissions, rejection of biomarker-driven claims, and potential inspection findings. Successful biomarker strategy requires documented interactions with agencies and incorporation of their feedback into development plans and regulatory submissions.

questions.question: Are discussions with agencies (e.g., biomarker qualification, CDx co-development) initiated or planned?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Regulatory Strategy Plan, Agency Interaction Plan, Meeting Request Dossiers (pre-IND, pre-CTA, scientific advice, FDA biomarker qualification program, FDA CDx pre-submission, EMA Innovation Task Force, PMDA consultations), meeting minutes, feedback responses, planning timelines, biomarker qualification submissions, CDx co-development documentation, governance review records, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Proactive engagement with regulatory agencies is essential for aligning biomarker qualification and CDx co-development with regulatory expectations. FDA, EMA, and PMDA provide structured pathways for biomarker qualification and device co-development. Failure to engage early results in late-stage regulatory surprises, delayed approvals, or rejected biomarker/CDx claims. A documented and proactive Agency Interaction Plan is now an expectation in regulatory inspections and due diligence reviews.

questions.question: Are biomarker data included in IND/CTA submissions or briefing books?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of Regulatory Strategy Plan, Clinical Development Plan, IND/CTA Table of Contents, briefing book templates and content, Module 2 and Module 5 content for biomarkers, biomarker validation reports, cross-references between clinical sections and biomarker sections, agency feedback records, alignment with FDA/EMA/PMDA guidance on biomarkers in submissions, governance review records, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies expect that biomarker data — if used in trial design, enrichment, or efficacy/safety assessment — is transparently included in IND/CTA submissions and briefing books. Lack of clear inclusion leads to regulatory questions, undermines credibility of biomarker-driven trial designs, and increases submission risk. Regulators expect to see biomarker validation, clinical rationale, and linkage to trial endpoints clearly documented and cross-referenced in submissions.

questions.question: If CDx is required, is an IVD partner identified and a development pathway in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of CDx Co-Development Plan, Regulatory Strategy Plan, IVD partner selection documents, due diligence reports, contractual agreements (e.g. MSA, CDA, development agreement), timelines for analytical/clinical validation, Design History File (DHF) initiation, IVDR or FDA PMA pathway documentation, global regulatory alignment strategy, cross-functional governance reviews, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: For programs requiring a companion diagnostic (CDx), regulatory agencies expect to see that a qualified IVD partner is identified and that a clear development pathway (FDA PMA, IDE; EU IVDR) is in place. Failure to align drug and CDx development timelines, or failure to identify a partner with necessary capabilities, leads to launch delays, regulatory rejection, and commercial risk. Many programs fail due to CDx strategy being an afterthought — it must be integrated and proactively managed with full documentation trail.

questions.question: Are nonclinical, clinical, and biomarker data integrated in a centralized database or data lake?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of data integration architecture and governance, existence and use of centralized data environments (data lake, translational platforms, integrated repositories), translational insight documentation in governance packs, decision records and dose/regimen decisions incorporating biomarker data, linkages between nonclinical-clinical-translational datasets, audit trails, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness
questions.rationale: Modern translational science and regulatory expectations require integrated, cross-domain decision-making supported by centralized and well-governed data. Siloed data or lack of integration reduces scientific insight, creates regulatory risk, and undermines strategic decisions (dose selection, go/no-go). Agencies expect to see evidence that translational data influences decision making and that governance processes leverage integrated data. Increasingly this is also a focus in due diligence and partner reviews.

questions.question: Are translational insights routinely discussed at governance or program review meetings?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of data integration architecture and governance, existence and use of centralized data environments (data lake, translational platforms, integrated repositories), translational insight documentation in governance packs, decision records and dose/regimen decisions incorporating biomarker data, linkages between nonclinical-clinical-translational datasets, audit trails, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness
questions.rationale: Modern translational science and regulatory expectations require integrated, cross-domain decision-making supported by centralized and well-governed data. Siloed data or lack of integration reduces scientific insight, creates regulatory risk, and undermines strategic decisions (dose selection, go/no-go). Agencies expect to see evidence that translational data influences decision making and that governance processes leverage integrated data. Increasingly this is also a focus in due diligence and partner reviews.

questions.question: Are biomarkers influencing go/no-go and dose/regimen decisions?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of data integration architecture and governance, existence and use of centralized data environments (data lake, translational platforms, integrated repositories), translational insight documentation in governance packs, decision records and dose/regimen decisions incorporating biomarker data, linkages between nonclinical-clinical-translational datasets, audit trails, QA oversight, TMF/ISF completeness
questions.rationale: Modern translational science and regulatory expectations require integrated, cross-domain decision-making supported by centralized and well-governed data. Siloed data or lack of integration reduces scientific insight, creates regulatory risk, and undermines strategic decisions (dose selection, go/no-go). Agencies expect to see evidence that translational data influences decision making and that governance processes leverage integrated data. Increasingly this is also a focus in due diligence and partner reviews.

questions.question: For advanced modalities (e.g., mRNA, cell therapy, radiopharma), are unique translational challenges addressed?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of modality-specific translational strategy (e.g., mRNA, gene therapy, cell therapy, radiopharma), modality-specific risk registers, clinical pharmacology and immunogenicity plans, biodistribution data, off-target risk assessment, durability assessments, platform comparability plans, assay validation for novel endpoints, governance review records, regulatory interaction records, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: Advanced modalities introduce unique translational challenges (e.g., delivery, immunogenicity, durability, off-target effects, integration, radiological risk) that must be proactively identified and addressed. Regulatory agencies now expect clear articulation of modality-specific translational risks and mitigation plans. Failure to address these challenges can lead to program delays, non-acceptance of IND/CTA, clinical holds, or safety risks. This is a key focus in due diligence for platform companies and emerging biotechs.

questions.question: Are immunogenicity, cellular persistence, or biodistribution biomarkers in development?
questions.rubric_tests: Are immunogenicity, cellular persistence, or biodistribution biomarkers in development?
questions.rationale: For advanced modalities (mRNA, cell therapy, gene therapy, radiopharma), regulators expect proactive development of modality-specific biomarkers to monitor key risks and mechanisms — particularly immunogenicity, cellular persistence, and biodistribution. Lack of validated biomarkers undermines safety monitoring, dose selection, and regulatory confidence. Sponsors must demonstrate that relevant biomarkers are in active development, with plans for validation, regulatory alignment, and clinical integration.

questions.question: Is platform data leveraged across programs to refine translational strategy?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of platform governance structure, cross-program data integration strategy, lessons learned documentation, formal feedback loops (program → platform), preclinical and clinical data reuse strategy, shared model libraries, biomarker cross-program insights, assay platform reuse, CMC platform strategy, regulatory precedent tracking, governance pack content, TMF/ISF completeness for platform-level data governance.
questions.rationale: A hallmark of leading-edge translational organizations is effective leveraging of platform learning across programs. Advanced modalities (mRNA, cell therapy, gene therapy, radiopharma) evolve rapidly — failure to cross-leverage data results in inefficiency, scientific gaps, and regulatory risk. Sponsors are expected to show how key translational insights (PK/PD, immunogenicity, biodistribution, safety signals, biomarkers, CMC data) are reused and iteratively improve program execution. Without formal platform governance and data integration, each program starts from scratch — an unsustainable model. Agencies also increasingly scrutinize platform consistency across programs.

questions.question: Is there an experienced translational medicine lead assigned?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of translational leadership structure, team composition and expertise matrix, external collaboration agreements and partner rosters, governance structure and decision-rights documentation, governance pack content, cross-functional integration evidence, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: The quality of translational strategy execution is heavily dependent on leadership, team expertise, external partnerships, and governance. Advanced modalities and modern biomarker-driven development require integrated, cross-disciplinary translational teams with strong governance and decision frameworks. Regulatory agencies and investors now routinely examine team capability, external network leverage, and governance rigor to assess program quality and risk. Without these foundations, translational strategies cannot be implemented effectively, leading to clinical risk, regulatory delay, and competitive disadvantage.

questions.question: Are team members skilled in biomarker science, modeling, systems biology, and clinical integration?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of translational leadership structure, team composition and expertise matrix, external collaboration agreements and partner rosters, governance structure and decision-rights documentation, governance pack content, cross-functional integration evidence, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: The quality of translational strategy execution is heavily dependent on leadership, team expertise, external partnerships, and governance. Advanced modalities and modern biomarker-driven development require integrated, cross-disciplinary translational teams with strong governance and decision frameworks. Regulatory agencies and investors now routinely examine team capability, external network leverage, and governance rigor to assess program quality and risk. Without these foundations, translational strategies cannot be implemented effectively, leading to clinical risk, regulatory delay, and competitive disadvantage.

questions.question: Are external collaborations (academia, CROs, biomarker consortia) in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of translational leadership structure, team composition and expertise matrix, external collaboration agreements and partner rosters, governance structure and decision-rights documentation, governance pack content, cross-functional integration evidence, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: The quality of translational strategy execution is heavily dependent on leadership, team expertise, external partnerships, and governance. Advanced modalities and modern biomarker-driven development require integrated, cross-disciplinary translational teams with strong governance and decision frameworks. Regulatory agencies and investors now routinely examine team capability, external network leverage, and governance rigor to assess program quality and risk. Without these foundations, translational strategies cannot be implemented effectively, leading to clinical risk, regulatory delay, and competitive disadvantage.

questions.question: Is there a fit-for-purpose governance and decision-making structure?
questions.rubric_tests: Review of translational leadership structure, team composition and expertise matrix, external collaboration agreements and partner rosters, governance structure and decision-rights documentation, governance pack content, cross-functional integration evidence, TMF/ISF completeness.
questions.rationale: The quality of translational strategy execution is heavily dependent on leadership, team expertise, external partnerships, and governance. Advanced modalities and modern biomarker-driven development require integrated, cross-disciplinary translational teams with strong governance and decision frameworks. Regulatory agencies and investors now routinely examine team capability, external network leverage, and governance rigor to assess program quality and risk. Without these foundations, translational strategies cannot be implemented effectively, leading to clinical risk, regulatory delay, and competitive disadvantage.
